narration.default: if-else gave you a way to pick between TWO paths. But what if you need to pick between FOUR — Grade A, B, C, or F? Or seven — days of the week? Or ten — different shop items? You CHAIN if-elses together using the special form "else if". C tries each condition in order from top to bottom, and the FIRST one that's true wins — the rest are skipped completely. The final else (no condition) catches everything that didn't match any of the previous conditions. It's like a flowchart with multiple decision diamonds in a row.

Lines 5 to 7 check the first condition: score >= 90 → print "Grade: A". If score is 90 or higher, this prints A and the entire chain STOPS — none of the else if blocks are even checked. This is important to understand: ORDER MATTERS in an if-else if chain. C goes top-down and picks the first match. If you put score >= 60 first, then EVERY score 60+ would match (because 95 is also 60+) and you'd never reach the more-specific A or B checks. So always go from MOST SPECIFIC to LEAST SPECIFIC, top to bottom.

Lines 7 to 11 chain three more checks: 75+ is B, 60+ is C, anything below those is F (the final else). Notice the structure: } else if (...) { ... } — the else and the if are joined together. You can chain as many else if blocks as you want — five, ten, twenty. After all the else ifs, the final else (no condition) is the catch-all that runs only when NONE of the previous conditions matched. Exactly ONE branch always runs — same guarantee as plain if-else, just with more options.

Score is 75. Trace it: 75 >= 90? NO. 75 >= 75? YES. So the second branch wins → "Grade: B" prints, and C skips the rest. Click Run to confirm. Now experiment: change score to 95 (should be A), then 65 (should be C), then 40 (should be F). Each different score triggers a different branch. This pattern — chaining checks from highest to lowest — is one of the most common shapes in real-world programs (grading systems, age groups, traffic light colors, anything with categories).

Imagine you accidentally put score >= 60 FIRST instead of score >= 90. What would happen? Every score 60 or higher would match the first condition and print "Grade: C" — even a 95 would print C, because C never gets to check the higher-grade conditions after the first match wins. So a top scorer would get a C. Bug! The fix is always: put your STRICTEST condition first (>= 90), then loosen as you go down. This is the most common bug in else-if chains. Read your conditions from top to bottom and ask: "is each one stricter than the next?" If not, reorder.
